01a_quotes.title: AI-prompted peer feedback: Can an AI feedback coach enhance peer feedback quality?

study_design: In a quasi-experimental design, students' peer feedback quality and their perception of the peer feedback process were analysed when taking part in a digital peer feedback activity.

In the first iteration (1), a quasi-experimental study was conducted to compare an intervention group (IG 1) with an enabled AI feedback coach with a control group (CG 1) without an AI feedback coach.
The use of two parallel groups in a competency course meant that the research design was quasi-experimental, and ecological validity was therefore prioritized over randomization.
task: Participants provided peers with feedback on their assignments.
After uploading the project report to the digital feedback environment, each student was tasked with providing peer feedback to two of their peers.
For each project report they provide feedback on, they must submit at least four feedback comments about what parts are good and what needs improvement.
In the feedback environment, they are presented with a document, in which they can highlight certain passages to connect their individual comments to.
After submitting their comments, they are then presented with a rubric to give an overall assessment of the project report within predefined criteria.
population: The study was conducted in a pedagogical course (3 ECTS) for PhD students who work as teaching assistants (TAs) within STEM disciplines at a Danish university.
Data was collected from PhD students enrolled in two iterations of the course.

In total, 46 students signed consent to participate in the study, with 20 students (38% female, 62% male, 0% non-binary, 0% undisclosed; average self-assessed peer feedback competence: 3.3; MAge: 27.61) in the IG1 and 26 students (43% female, 53% male, 0% non-binary, 3% undisclosed; average self-assessed peer feedback competence: 3.2; MAge: 27.87) in the CG1.

In the second iteration of the course (2), the group of PhD students only took part in the peer feedback activity with the AI feedback coach enabled. 62 students (40% female, 57% male, 2% non-binary, 2% undisclosed; average self-assessed peer feedback competence: 3.4, MAge: 27.47) signed consent.
arms: In the first iteration, an intervention group had access to the AI feedback coach enabled in the digital feedback environment, while the control group had the same task description and digital environment, except for the lack of the AI feedback coach.
In the second iteration, participants took part in the same intervention as the IG of the first iteration (AI feedback coach enabled).
outcome_definitions: Feedback comments from participants were analyzed concerning content, text complexity, and sentiment.

The feedback comments were analysed using an adapted version of Prins et al.'s (2006) feedback quality index. The feedback quality index assesses feedback comments according to a set of criteria and evaluates whether the feedback is of 'good', 'average', or 'sub-optimal' feedback quality. The following criteria were used to code the feedback comments: Content/Criteria, Explanations/Examples, Specificity, Questions, Advice/Suggestions, First-person (Style), and Encouragement.

The same 396 feedback comments were also analysed concerning composition, readability, and sentiment. The analysis utilized the Python packages TextDescriptives and VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner) sentiment analysis, which provide ready-to-use algorithms for calculating the relevant descriptive values based on the supplied text input.
Survey responses were coded for sentiment and themes.
statistical_results: In total, participants wrote 396 feedback comments.

The analyses of differences in feedback quality between participants of the CG and IG showed that members of the IG included significantly fewer reflective questions, t(44)= 2.155, p=.031, d=.217, and their feedback was significantly less criteria-based, t(44)= 2.014, p=.044, d=0.203.
Analyses showed no significant differences between the CG and IG concerning the sentiment score. Both means were found to be positive (MIG=0.460, MCG=0.467).
IG members used significantly shorter sentences, t(44)=3.274, p=.001, d=0.7, and used fewer tokens per comment, t(44)= 2.285, p=.024, d=0.491.
Furthermore, IG members had a significantly higher count of syllables per token, t(44=-4.969, p<0.001, d=1.108).

Members of the IG wrote significantly more complex feedback comments, as measured by several measures for text complexity. These metrics are 'Flesch Reading Ease', t(44)=3.331, p=.001, d=0.739, 'SMOG', t(44)=-2.459, p=.016, d=0.539, 'Coleman Liau', t(44)=-4.437, p<.001, d=0.985, and 'Lix', t(44)=-2.505, p=.014, d=0.551.
A total of 25 answers were coded as 'positive' or 'somewhat positive', while 11 were neutral, and 24 were 'negative' or 'somewhat negative'.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Results show adverse effects of the AI feedback coach.
Intervention group participants' feedback included fewer reflective questions and adhered less to criteria. They provided shorter, more complex feedback.
Students indicated mixed views on the AI feedback coach, with some finding it helpful and others distracting.
A notable subset of students stated overreliance on the AI coach, prioritizing its validation over their own judgment.
Results suggest that AI tools need thoughtful integration, possibly with additional scaffolding to counteract overreliance and avoid negative impact on peer feedback quality and feedback experience.

As the AI is a proprietary model, and we do not have full access to the prompts and pre-prompting that the system utilised, the interactions with the AI could not be systematically controlled or recorded. Therefore, it is challenging to fully understand the potential impact of prompt phrasing on the observed outcomes.

Though the course had over 60 students in each group, the sample size of the final groups was unfortunately quite low, as less than half of the students consented to have their feedback analyzed. This means that the smaller sample size could impact generalizability.
The design means that causality cannot be firmly established, but as a trade-off, the findings are likely more applicable to typical educational environments.
Unfortunately, the study design did not include a pre and post-test, which means that the development of subjects could not be analysed in depth.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical quasi-experimental study with two iterations. The first iteration compared an intervention group (IG1, n=20) with AI feedback coach enabled to a control group (CG1, n=26) without the AI coach. The second iteration (n=62) only included the intervention condition. The study uses mixed methods, combining quantitative analysis of feedback quality, text metrics, and sentiment with qualitative thematic analysis of student survey responses. The paper reports adverse effects of the AI feedback coach on peer feedback quality, particularly regarding reflective questions and adherence to criteria, along with mixed student perceptions.

01_extraction.title: AI-prompted peer feedback: Can an AI feedback coach enhance peer feedback quality?
01_extraction.study_design_description: In a quasi-experimental design, students' peer feedback quality and their perception of the peer feedback process were analysed when taking part in a digital peer feedback activity. In the first iteration, a quasi-experimental study was conducted to compare an intervention group (IG 1) with an enabled AI feedback coach with a control group (CG 1) without an AI feedback coach. The use of two parallel groups in a competency course meant that the research design was quasi-experimental, and ecological validity was therefore prioritized over randomization. In the second iteration of the course, the group of PhD students only took part in the peer feedback activity with the AI feedback coach enabled.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Participants provided peers with feedback on their assignments. After uploading the project report to the digital feedback environment, each student was tasked with providing peer feedback to two of their peers. For each project report they provide feedback on, they must submit at least four feedback comments about what parts are good and what needs improvement. In the feedback environment, they are presented with a document, in which they can highlight certain passages to connect their individual comments to. After submitting their comments, they are then presented with a rubric to give an overall assessment of the project report within predefined criteria.
01_extraction.population_description: The study was conducted in a pedagogical course (3 ECTS) for PhD students who work as teaching assistants (TAs) within STEM disciplines at a Danish university. Data was collected from PhD students enrolled in two iterations of the course.

arms.description: In the first iteration, an intervention group had access to the AI feedback coach enabled in the digital feedback environment.
In the first iteration, the control group had the same task description and digital environment, except for the lack of the AI feedback coach.
In the second iteration, participants took part in the same intervention as the IG of the first iteration (AI feedback coach enabled).

The feedback comments were analysed concerning sentiment. The analysis utilized the Python packages TextDescriptives and VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner) sentiment analysis, which provide ready-to-use algorithms for calculating the relevant descriptive values based on the supplied text input.
The feedback comments were analysed concerning text complexity, specifically sentence length.

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: The analyses of differences in feedback quality between participants of the CG and IG showed that members of the IG included significantly fewer reflective questions, t(44)= 2.155, p=.031, d=.217
their feedback was significantly less criteria-based, t(44)= 2.014, p=.044, d=0.203
Analyses showed no significant differences between the CG and IG concerning the sentiment score. Both means were found to be positive (MIG=0.460, MCG=0.467).
IG members used significantly shorter sentences, t(44)=3.274, p=.001, d=0.7
used fewer tokens per comment, t(44)= 2.285, p=.024, d=0.491
Furthermore, IG members had a significantly higher count of syllables per token, t(44=-4.969, p<0.001, d=1.108).
These metrics are 'Flesch Reading Ease', t(44)=3.331, p=.001, d=0.739
A total of 25 answers were coded as 'positive' or 'somewhat positive', while 11 were neutral, and 24 were 'negative' or 'somewhat negative'.

01_extraction.notes: This is a quasi-experimental study with two iterations. Iteration 1 compared intervention group (IG1, n=20) with AI feedback coach enabled to control group (CG1, n=26) without AI coach. Iteration 2 (n=62) included only the intervention condition with AI coach enabled. The study reports adverse effects of the AI feedback coach on peer feedback quality: IG members included significantly fewer reflective questions and their feedback was significantly less criteria-based. IG members also used significantly shorter sentences, fewer tokens per comment, but had higher syllables per token and more complex feedback as measured by multiple text complexity metrics (Flesch Reading Ease, SMOG, Coleman Liau, Lix). No significant differences were found in sentiment scores between groups. Survey responses showed mixed views on the AI feedback coach. The paper notes that as the AI is a proprietary model with limited access to prompts, interactions could not be systematically controlled or recorded. Sample size was lower than ideal due to consent rates (less than half of students consented to have their feedback analyzed). The quasi-experimental design prioritized ecological validity over randomization, and causality cannot be firmly established. The study design did not include a pre- and post-test.

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly states it used a 'quasi-experimental design' comparing an intervention group with AI feedback coach to a control group without it, and notes that 'ecological validity was therefore prioritized over randomization.' This is a quasi-experimental comparison without random assignment, fitting the natural_experiment category.

per_outcome.operationalization: Count or score of question-type feedback comments using adapted Prins et al. (2006) feedback quality index criteria
Score of content/criteria-focused feedback comments using adapted Prins et al. (2006) feedback quality index
VADER sentiment analysis score computed from feedback comment text using Python TextDescriptives package
Average number of words per sentence in feedback comments, computed via text analysis
Average number of tokens per feedback comment, computed via text analysis
Average number of syllables per token in feedback comments, computed via text analysis
Flesch Reading Ease readability score computed from feedback comment text complexity
SMOG readability index computed from feedback comment text complexity
Coleman-Liau readability index computed from feedback comment text complexity
Lix readability index computed from feedback comment text complexity
Coded sentiment from open-ended survey responses about the feedback experience

04_onet.onet_label: Interpreting the Meaning of Information for Others
04_onet.rationale: The participants' substantive task is 'providing peer feedback' where they evaluate project reports, highlight passages, submit comments about strengths and weaknesses, and assess work against rubric criteria. This is fundamentally about interpreting academic work and communicating its meaning/quality to peers. While the participants are PhD students serving as teaching assistants, the measured activity is peer feedback provision (not their TA duties or the researchers' experimental design). WA-19 'Interpreting the Meaning of Information for Others' captures this evaluative communication work better than WA-16 'Training and Teaching Others' (too broad for this specific feedback task) or occupation 25-1199.00 'Postsecondary Teachers, All Other' (would require measuring their full TA role, not just peer feedback).

alternates.onet_label: Judging the Qualities of Objects, Services, or People

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Selected intervention_group_1_ai_enabled as the AI arm (n=20) vs control_group_1_no_ai as the human-only baseline (n=26). This is the only direct comparison reported in the statistics.
llm_extracted.notes: The verbatim quote states 'their feedback was significantly less criteria-based' for the AI group, which suggests the AI group had LOWER criteria-based feedback. However, the positive d=0.203 and positive t=2.014 indicate the AI group had HIGHER values. The language may be describing the control group. Given the statistical direction (positive t and d), I'm treating larger values as better (more criteria-based feedback). The comparison is between iteration 1 intervention (AI) vs iteration 1 control (no AI).
source_quotes: their feedback was significantly less criteria-based, t(44)= 2.014, p=.044, d=0.203

computed.notes: The verbatim quote states 'their feedback was significantly less criteria-based' for the AI group, which suggests the AI group had LOWER criteria-based feedback. However, the positive d=0.203 and positive t=2.014 indicate the AI group had HIGHER values. The language may be describing the control group. Given the statistical direction (positive t and d), I'm treating larger values as better (more criteria-based feedback). The comparison is between iteration 1 intervention (AI) vs iteration 1 control (no AI).
